did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning models (ResNet-50, VGG-16, XceptionNet, and EfficientNetB3) for skin lesion classification (benign vs. malignant) using the SLICE-3D dataset. It also employed Explainable AI (XAI) techniques, specifically Grad-CAM, to visualize decision-making and enhance interpretability for dermatological diagnosis.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the implementation and comparison of four pre-trained deep learning models (ResNet-50, VGG-16, XceptionNet, and EfficientNetB3) on the SLICE-3D dataset for skin lesion classification. It includes specific methodology sections (hyperparameters, Grad-CAM implementation), results (accuracy metrics, confusion matrices), and a discussion of findings, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Enhancing Skin Lesion Diagnosis: Model Explainability through Grad-CAM
journal: 2026 Second International Conference on Intelligent Systems for Communication, IoT and Security (ICISCoIS)
brief_summary: This study enhances skin lesion diagnosis by integrating Explainable AI (XAI) with deep learning models to classify benign versus malignant lesions using the SLICE-3D dataset. The researchers implemented and compared four pre-trained Convolutional Neural Networks: ResNet-50, VGG-16, XceptionNet, and EfficientNetB3. To address the 'black box' nature of these models, Grad-CAM was employed to visualize the specific regions of the input images that influenced the classification decisions. The performance of the models was evaluated using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with EfficientNetB3 achieving the highest accuracy of 98.21%. The Grad-CAM visualizations demonstrated that the model focused on clinically relevant dermatological features, such as lesion borders and color variations, aligning with the ABCDE rule for melanoma detection. The findings suggest that combining high-performance deep learning with XAI techniques improves model interpretability and trustworthiness for clinical decision support.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task using the SLICE-3D dataset. The model achieved 98.21% accuracy, 90.00% precision, 91.00% recall, and 90.00% F1-Score for this class.

One of the two classes in the binary classification task using the SLICE-3D dataset. The model achieved 89.00% precision, 88.00% recall, and 88.00% F1-Score for this class. The study notes a potential data imbalance where there are more benign cases matched to malignant ones.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign or malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin lesion classification' using the SLICE-3D dataset comprising 'benign and malignant skin lesion samples'. The model outputs class probabilities for two categories (benign and malignant) using a softmax function and categorical cross-entropy loss, which is appropriate for binary classification. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: SLICE-3D (Malignant vs. Benign)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing high-resolution dermoscopic images of skin spots, sorted into benign and malignant categories. The paper refers to it as a tuned version of the SLICE-3D dataset named 'Malignant vs. Benign'.

use.training: The dataset was split into training and testing parts. The training set was further split into training and validation steps. Image augmentation was applied to the training data to mitigate dataset imbalance and increase miscellany.
use.validation: Used for validation steps during the training process (40 epochs, batch size 32) to monitor performance and prevent overfitting.
use.testing: Used for final performance evaluation, including confusion matrices, ROC curves, and Grad-CAM visualizations. The model was evaluated on 660 data points.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices, without statistical significance testing.

SLICE-3D (Malignant vs. Benign).experiment: Comparison of four pre-trained CNN models (ResNet-50, VGG-16, XceptionNet, EfficientNetB3)
SLICE-3D (Malignant vs. Benign).notes: EfficientNetB3 achieved the highest accuracy among the compared models. The study notes a potential data imbalance with more benign cases than malignant ones. Grad-CAM visualizations confirmed the model focused on clinically relevant features like lesion borders and color variations.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNetB3 (Primary), with comparisons to ResNet-50, VGG-16, and XceptionNet
architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning from pre-trained models on large datasets (specifically mentioned for EfficientNetB3)

modifications: Suspension of top few layers of the original structure
architecture_details.output_head: Softmax function for binary classification (benign vs malignant)

callbacks: Adjusting Learning Rate (dynamic adjustment based on accuracy threshold of 0.9 and validation loss)
Model Saving (saving weights with lowest validation loss)
training_details.data_augmentation: Simulating differences in skin lesions (lighting conditions, rotations, scales)

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to 224x224 pixels using TensorFlow's ImageDataGenerator
Scaling pixel values to the range [0, 1] for normalization
Data augmentation via horizontal flipping on the training set
Simulating differences in skin lesions such as lighting conditions, rotations, and scales
Custom scalar preprocessing to preserve original image characteristics

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the entire training set using TensorFlow's ImageDataGenerator to increase robustness and prevent overfitting.
Used to mitigate dataset imbalance and increase the miscellany of training data, simulating differences in skin lesion lighting.
Used to simulate differences in skin lesion orientations during augmentation.
Used to simulate differences in skin lesion sizes/scales during augmentation.

conclusions: EfficientNetB3 achieved the highest accuracy (98.21%) among the tested pre-trained CNN models (ResNet-50, VGG-16, XceptionNet, and EfficientNetB3) for skin lesion classification on the SLICE-3D dataset.

The integration of Grad-CAM as an Explainable AI technique successfully visualized the regions of interest, demonstrating that the model focused on clinically relevant dermatological features such as lesion borders and color variations, aligning with the ABCDE rule for melanoma detection.
The hybrid approach of combining high-performance deep learning with XAI enhances model interpretability and trustworthiness, supporting dermatologists in efficient investigation and diagnosis.

The study acknowledges limitations including potential data imbalance (more benign cases than malignant), limited diversity in skin types and lesion variability which may affect generalizability, and the reliance on a single dataset.

Grad-CAM visualizations help validate model decisions by highlighting relevant clinical features and disregarding irrelevant background areas, thereby improving the potential integration of AI predictions into clinical diagnostic workflows.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a potential data imbalance in the SLICE-3D dataset, with more benign cases than malignant ones, which poses a risk of biased model performance favoring the prevalent class.
The dataset has limited diversity in terms of skin types and lesion variability, which may reduce the model's generalizability to larger or different populations.
The reliance on a single dataset (SLICE-3D) means the results may not extend to other datasets with different characteristics, limiting external validation.
While Grad-CAM provides visual insights, the paper notes that these explanations do not guarantee alignment with clinically relevant features, potentially limiting their clinical utility.

The paper reports conflicting accuracy figures for the EfficientNetB3 model: 98.21% in the Abstract and Table III, but 91.2% in Section IV.F, indicating a significant inconsistency in reported results.
The study is limited to binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not address multi-class skin cancer diagnosis or other lesion types.
The research was conducted using a tuned version of the SLICE-3D dataset named 'Malignant vs. Benign', rather than the original full dataset, which may affect comparability with other studies.
